title: Stand up to US on AI, says Turnbull
full_text:
Former prime minister Malcolm Turnbull says Australia must stand up to the US and insist local authorities and businesses get access to the latest artificial intelligence models, as one of his former advisers was hired by OpenAI to head up regional security policy.
The company behind ChatGPT has appointed Peter Anstee to lead its national security policy team across the Asia Pacific, which he will do from Singapore.
He advised Turnbull before being a founding director at cybersecurity firm CyberCX, and then first assistant secretary at the departments of home affairs and prime minister and cabinet.
Anstee's appointment comes as the Trump administration restricts the latest AI models from OpenAI and Anthropic to select groups of American companies.
This has left Australia and other allies waiting for US approval to use the systems to plug their own cyber flaws, and the AI companies with regional public relations problems.
"The more the Americans flex and use their power to discriminate against other countries, even close allies like Australia, the more people will start looking for sovereign solutions, or at least non-American solutions," Turnbull said.
"Given the relationship we have and the fact we are supposed to have a level playing field, we should be insisting on access [to new AI platforms].
"The government will be worried about getting a knock-back, but at some point Australian governments have got to remember that the primary strategic objective is defending Australia, not to avoid upsetting the Americans."
Anstee was a senior adviser to Turnbull from 2015 to 2018, leading key areas related to foreign affairs and national security. This included cybersecurity, and the controversial decision to ban Chinese companies such as Huawei from providing equipment to Australia's 5G mobile networks.
Anstee said his role would focus on helping shape how frontier AI was understood, governed and secured across the Asia-Pacific region. This would mean working with governments, industry partners and civil society on the opportunities and challenges that come with rapidly advancing technology.
"AI and geopolitics are deeply intertwined ... We want to see regional norms shaped by principles of AI democratisation, empowerment and universal prosperity," he said. "I've spent much of my career working at the intersection of government, technology and national security, and I see this role as bringing those perspectives together at a time when AI is becoming one of the defining policy issues of our time."
Earlier this month, Anthropic said it would extend access to its Mythos model to Australian organisations as part of a staggered release aimed at fixing security vulnerabilities before the powerful product potentially fell into the hands of hackers.
But the Trump administration issued an order to prevent non-US citizens accessing it or a supposedly safer version called Fable 5.
At the weekend OpenAI said it was releasing a preview version of its new GPT-5.6 model series to a small group of trusted partners whose names had been approved by the US government.
Anstee declined to comment on the issue of access for Australians, but an OpenAI spokeswoman said the company did not believe narrow access should become the default for new models. She said this approach delayed essential access for the users, developers, enterprises and cyber defenders.
When Mythos first launched, Australia's former top cybersecurity boss Alastair MacGibbon said it "crossed the Rubicon" by making organisations' processes for plugging cyber flaws obsolete.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Paul Smith | Technology
heading: Data Science and AI
